| **Suno v3** | **2024-03** | **Suno** | **consumer quality** | **product** | **2 min** |
| Udio v1 | 2024-04 | Udio | competitive | product | 2 min |
| Suno v4 | 2024-11 | Suno | improved | product | 4+ min |

Suno's product velocity through 2024 outpaced research labs significantly. Whether this advantage holds depends on the copyright lawsuits and on whether Big Tech labs (OpenAI, Google, Meta) eventually ship competitive consumer products.

## The copyright case

In June 2024, RIAA-affiliated labels (Sony Music, Universal Music Group, Warner Music) filed lawsuits against Suno and Udio in US federal court alleging willful copyright infringement during training. Suno's response (filed August 2024) acknowledged training on copyrighted music but argued fair-use defense. The case is procedurally pending as of 2026 and is one of the most-watched AI copyright cases globally — the outcome affects training-data strategies across the entire generative AI industry.

nodes.title: DBRX — Databricks' Open MoE

model_spec.architecture: Sparse MoE — 16 experts × 8.25B per expert, top-4 routing

public_view.plain_english:
Databricks' open-weight MoE model released March 2024 — the
first major US open release after the Mixtral / Llama / Mistral
wave. 132B total parameters, 36B active per query, optimized
for serving on Databricks' own infrastructure. Notable for
being trained on 12T tokens (the largest training-data
disclosure for a US open model at the time).
public_view.analogy:
"Databricks the AI company" announcement — the model was the
visible artifact, but the strategic message was that
Databricks (a $43B-valued data-warehouse company) was now a
serious AI infrastructure provider, not just a customer.
public_view.investment_angle:
DBRX was the model artifact behind Databricks' $1.3B Mosaic AI
acquisition strategy paying off. Operationally, DBRX wasn't
market-leading — by Q3 2024 it was clearly behind Llama 3,
Qwen 2, and DeepSeek V2 — but as a strategic
"we ship frontier models" demonstration it justified
Databricks' AI-platform positioning. The company crossed
$3B ARR by late 2024, with ~25% attributed to AI workloads
that DBRX helped seed.
public_view.why_it_matters:
DBRX was the moment "data company" and "AI model lab" became
overlapping categories. Snowflake (Arctic, April 2024) and
BigQuery's AI investments followed similar logic: own the
data, own the model that runs on the data.

nodes.body_md:
## What it is

A sparse Mixture-of-Experts decoder-only transformer with 16 fine-grained experts (8.25B params each) and top-4 routing per token. 132B total parameters, 36B active per forward pass. Trained on 12T tokens of English text + code. Released open-weight under a custom Databricks license (commercial use OK, with conditions for >700M MAU services).

## DBRX vs concurrent open models (Q1-Q2 2024)

| Model | Date | Active params | MMLU | HumanEval | License |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Llama 2 70B | 2023-07 | 70B | 68.9% | 29.9% | LLAMA 2 |
| Mixtral 8x7B | 2023-12 | 13B | 70.6% | 40.2% | Apache 2.0 |
| Mixtral 8x22B | 2024-04 | 39B | 77.8% | 45.1% | Apache 2.0 |
| **DBRX** | **2024-03** | **36B** | **73.7%** | **70.1%** | **DBRX (commercial OK)** |
| Llama 3 70B | 2024-04 | 70B | 82.0% | 81.7% | Llama 3 |
| Qwen 1.5 72B | 2024-02 | 72B | 77.5% | 64.6% | Qwen |
| Snowflake Arctic | 2024-04 | 17B (480B total) | 67.3% | 64.3% | Apache 2.0 |

DBRX held the open-source leaderboard for ~3 weeks before Llama 3 70B's release. Its architecture (fine-grained MoE) influenced subsequent open releases including Qwen 2-MoE and DeepSeek V2.

nodes.title: Stable Audio 2.0 — Open Latent-Diffusion Audio

model_spec.architecture: Latent diffusion on EnCodec audio tokens + DiT (Diffusion Transformer) backbone

benchmarks.name: Output duration
benchmarks.vs_baseline: Stable Audio 1.0 90s; MusicGen 30s; Suno v3 (closed) 2 min

public_view.plain_english:
Stability AI's open text-to-audio generator, releasing 3-minute
44.1kHz stereo tracks from text prompts. Less viral than Suno
(which had launched v3 two weeks earlier as a polished consumer
product), but the only open-weight option in the category — a
role analogous to what Stable Diffusion played in image
generation while DALL-E was closed.
public_view.analogy:
The "Stable Diffusion of audio" — same Stability AI playbook
of "release open weights as the open-source counterweight to
closed competitors." Less polished consumer experience, more
foundational ecosystem value.
public_view.investment_angle:
Stable Audio 2 was Stability AI's attempt to repeat its
image-generation playbook in audio. The execution
underperformed: by 2024 Stability AI was in financial
distress (founder Emad Mostaque had departed in March 2024;
funding crises continued through 2024-25). The open-weight
audio category is now smaller commercially than Suno / Udio's
closed business, illustrating that open weights aren't
automatically the dominant strategy in every modality.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Stable Audio 2 is the open baseline that makes the closed
audio-AI category contestable. Without it, Suno / Udio /
ElevenLabs would have unchallenged pricing power.

nodes.body_md:
## What it is

A latent diffusion model for audio generation. The audio is encoded into discrete tokens via EnCodec (Meta's neural audio codec), and a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) denoises the token sequence conditioned on text. Released open-weight under a non-commercial license, with a paid commercial license available from Stability AI.

## Stable Audio 2 vs concurrent text-to-audio (April 2024)

| Model | Date | Org | Open? | Length | Quality |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jukebox | 2020 | OpenAI | research | minutes | early-era |
| MusicLM | 2023-01 | Google | demos | 30s | research preview |
| MusicGen | 2023-06 | Meta | open | 30s | open baseline |
| Stable Audio 1.0 | 2023-09 | Stability | open | 90s | open improvement |
| Suno v3 | 2024-03 | Suno | product | 2 min | consumer breakthrough |
| **Stable Audio 2.0** | **2024-04** | **Stability AI** | **open** | **3 min** | **best open at release** |
| Udio v1 | 2024-04 | Udio | product | 2 min | competitive consumer |
| Suno v4 | 2024-11 | Suno | product | 4+ min | continued lead |

nodes.title: Cohere Command R+ — RAG-Optimized Open Frontier

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer + native tool use + multilingual + RAG-optimized post-training

benchmarks.name: RAG benchmark (HotpotQA, NQ)
benchmarks.score: best open-weight RAG quality at release
benchmarks.vs_baseline: outperforms Llama 2 70B by ~15pts on multi-hop QA

benchmarks.name: Tool use accuracy
benchmarks.score: first open model with strong native tool use

public_view.plain_english:
Cohere's flagship open-weight model, released April 2024.
Differentiated by being explicitly trained for retrieval-
augmented generation (RAG) workflows — given retrieved
documents in context, it integrates them more accurately and
cites them more reliably than generic LLMs. The default model
behind much enterprise RAG infrastructure in 2024-25.
public_view.analogy:
The "specialist B2B player" of the open-LLM market — where
Llama is the consumer-AI ecosystem star, Command R+ is the
enterprise-RAG workhorse. Less viral, more profitable per
deployment.

applications.product: Embedded in Notion AI / Box AI / Oracle / others (enterprise)
Hosted on Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (deep partnership)

public_view.investment_angle:
Cohere's positioning as the "enterprise-trusted alternative to
OpenAI/Anthropic" was bought partly with Command R+'s release.
The company raised $500M Series D at $5.5B valuation in July
2024 partly on this positioning. Strategic moat: enterprise
relationships (Oracle, Salesforce, Notion) + multilingual
coverage that Western-focused competitors deprioritize +
explicit on-prem deployment options that the OpenAI / Anthropic
APIs don't offer. By 2025-26 the company's revenue trajectory
(reportedly $300M+ ARR) was solid but smaller than the
flagship-lab tier — illustrating the tradeoff between
differentiated B2B positioning and consumer-attention scale.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Command R+ is the proof that "specialized for enterprise"
can be a viable LLM-business positioning distinct from
"race to the frontier." The continued existence of Cohere
as a meaningful enterprise vendor (vs being absorbed by
a hyperscaler) validates the segment.

nodes.body_md:
## What it is

A 104B-parameter decoder-only transformer released open-weight (CC-BY-NC-4.0) on April 4, 2024. Architecturally not novel — Cohere's contributions were in the post-training pipeline:

1. **RAG-optimized fine-tuning** — heavy emphasis on producing accurate citations and integrating retrieved context faithfully
2. **Native tool use** — first-class support for function calling with structured argument schemas
3. **Multilingual coverage** — 10 languages with strong fluency (English, French, Spanish, Italian, German, Portuguese, Japanese, Korean, Arabic, Chinese)
4. **Explicit on-prem deployability** — model card includes detailed compute requirements for self-hosting

## Command R+ vs concurrent peers (Q2 2024)

| Model | Date | Params | RAG quality | Tool use | License |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4 Turbo | 2023-11 | undisclosed | strong | strong | API |
| Claude 3 Opus | 2024-03 | undisclosed | strong | added later | API |
| **Command R+** | **2024-04** | **104B** | **best open** | **native** | **open (CC-BY-NC)** |
| Llama 3 70B | 2024-04 | 70B | RAG via prompt | needs scaffolding | open commercial |
| Mixtral 8x22B | 2024-04 | 141B | OK | needs scaffolding | Apache 2.0 |

Command R+'s niche — best-in-class open RAG + tool use — was the differentiator. Generic-quality benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval) were strong but not leading.

## The Cohere strategic position

Cohere is the only major LLM lab founded by Google Brain alumni (Aidan Gomez, an author of "Attention Is All You Need") that explicitly chose enterprise B2B positioning over consumer-AI. The company has stayed relatively small (~300 employees by 2025) and avoided consumer-product launches, focusing exclusively on the B2B funnel. By 2026, this positioning has produced a stable but smaller business than the flagship labs — which Cohere's leadership has framed as deliberate.

nodes.title: Llama 3 — Meta's Frontier-Comparable Open Model

model_spec.architecture: Llama 2 + grouped-query attention (all sizes), 128k vocab
model_spec.training_tokens: 15T (>5× Chinchilla-optimal — deliberate over-training for inference efficiency)

public_view.plain_english:
Meta's third-generation open-weight model, trained on 15 trillion
tokens — over 7× more than Llama 2. The 70B variant matched or
approached GPT-4 and Claude 3 Sonnet on most benchmarks while
being downloadable for free with a permissive license. Followed
in July 2024 by Llama 3.1 with a 405B version that explicitly
targeted GPT-4 / Claude 3.5 parity.
public_view.analogy:
The first open-weight model that could plausibly replace a closed
frontier API in production use cases — like the moment Linux
became viable for serious enterprise workloads, but for AI.

applications.product: WhatsApp / Instagram / Facebook Meta AI assistant
Together AI, Anyscale, Fireworks hosted endpoints

public_view.investment_angle:
Llama 3 is the strongest evidence to date that the closed-vs-open
gap is closing, not widening. Meta spent $20B+ in 2024 capex to
ship a model it gave away — a strategy that only makes sense if
open weights are a defensive moat against OpenAI/Microsoft and
Google (denying them pricing power) and an offensive distribution
play (Meta AI is now embedded in WhatsApp / Instagram with 3B+ MAU).
Drove inference-pricing collapse: Llama 3 70B served at <$1 per
1M output tokens by mid-2024, vs Claude Opus at $75. The "open
pulls down closed prices" thesis became operating reality.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Llama 3's release cadence and quality made open weights the
structural baseline for LLM economics. Any closed model now has
to justify its premium against a free open alternative within
one to two quarters of release. This pricing pressure is the
single biggest factor reshaping the LLM business in 2024-26.

nodes.body_md:
## What it is

Two foundation models (8B and 70B) at launch, with a 405B variant added in Llama 3.1 (2024-07-23). All variants share architecture: a Llama 2-style decoder-only transformer with GQA, RoPE, RMSNorm, SwiGLU, but with a much larger vocabulary (128k tokens vs 32k in Llama 2) and longer training (15T tokens vs 2T).

The deliberate over-training (15T tokens for an 8B model is ~1875 tok/param vs Chinchilla's ~20 tok/param) is the most consequential design decision: it sacrifices training-time efficiency for inference-time efficiency. Modern serving economics make this worthwhile.

## Llama 3 vs concurrent peers (Q2 2024)

| Model | Date | Org | Params | MMLU | HumanEval | GSM8K | License | Cost / 1M out |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4 Turbo | 2023-11 | OpenAI | undisclosed | ~86% | ~85% | — | API | $30 |
| Claude 3 Opus | 2024-03 | Anthropic | undisclosed | 86.8% | 84.9% | 95.0% | API | $75 |
| Gemini 1.5 Pro | 2024-02 | Google | undisclosed | 83.7% | 71.9% | 91.7% | API | $21 |
| **Llama 3 70B** | **2024-04** | **Meta** | **70B** | **82.0%** | **81.7%** | **93.0%** | **open** | **~$0.90 (Together)** |
| Llama 3.1 405B | 2024-07 | Meta | 405B | 88.6% | 89.0% | 96.8% | open | ~$5 (Together) |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet | 2024-06 | Anthropic | undisclosed | 88.7% | 92.0% | 96.4% | API | $15 |

The 405B variant approaches Claude 3.5 Sonnet quality at one-third the price (when self-hosted via Together / Fireworks). For high-volume workloads this is the operative comparison.

## What 15T tokens of training data looks like

Meta's data card disclosure:
- **>5% non-English** (vs <2% for prior open models)
- **>4% code** in pretraining (Code Llama-style)
- ~50% deduplicated CommonCrawl
- Quality filtering using Llama 2 itself as a classifier

The data composition + scale is the technical moat. Meta does not disclose the specific quality filters, which are widely considered the highest-leverage piece.

## Llama 3.1 405B — the open frontier

Released July 23, 2024 with Meta's "Open Source AI Is the Path Forward" manifesto from Mark Zuckerberg. Specs:
- 405B params, 16k context (extended from 8k)
- Trained on H100 GPUs, ~16k of them, ~30M GPU-hours total
- First open-weight model whose training compute exceeded GPT-4's estimated training compute
- Cost to host: ~$5/1M output tokens at Together AI launch pricing

405B is the line in the sand: open weights at GPT-4-class scale, free to download, free to commercial-use (subject to the >700M MAU carve-out).

nodes.title: Phi-3 — Microsoft's Small Models, Big Quality

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer trained on heavily-filtered + synthetic data

benchmarks.name: Inference: tokens / second on iPhone 14
benchmarks.vs_baseline: first GPT-3.5-class model practical on consumer phones

public_view.plain_english:
Microsoft's series of small foundation models — 3.8B / 7B / 14B
parameters — that punched far above their weight by training on
aggressively filtered "textbook-quality" data plus synthetic
examples generated by GPT-4. The 3.8B Mini variant reaches
GPT-3.5-class quality while running on a smartphone — making
it the foundation of much on-device AI in 2024-25.
public_view.analogy:
The "data quality is the moat, not parameter count" thesis —
proved at scale. Where DeepSeek's frame is "smarter
architecture," Phi's frame is "smarter data." Both reduce the
importance of raw compute as the determinant of capability.
public_view.investment_angle:
Phi-3 validated the on-device AI investment thesis that
Apple, Qualcomm, and Microsoft were all betting on with
Copilot+ PC, Apple Intelligence, and Snapdragon X. The
model line also gave Microsoft a counter-narrative to the
"OpenAI is doing all the AI work for Microsoft" framing —
Phi was Microsoft Research's own published-paper
contribution, demonstrating internal capability beyond the
OpenAI partnership. Subsequent Phi-3.5 and Phi-4 (2024-25)
extended the methodology.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Phi-3 is the proof point that small models can be useful.
The "small specialized model" segment (Phi, Gemma, Llama
3.2-1B/3B, Qwen 0.5B/1.5B, Apple's on-device models)
is now a real category, and Phi was the trigger.

nodes.body_md:
## What it is

Three foundation models from Microsoft Research, released April 23, 2024:

| Variant | Params | Context | Distinction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phi-3 Mini** | 3.8B | 4k / 128k | runs on smartphone |
| **Phi-3 Small** | 7B | 8k | mid-tier on-device |
| **Phi-3 Medium** | 14B | 4k / 128k | desktop / laptop tier |

Architecturally standard decoder-only transformers. The contribution is **training data**: 3.3T tokens consisting of heavily filtered web pages + heavily filtered code + synthetic "textbook quality" data generated by larger models (GPT-3.5 / GPT-4). The data-curation pipeline is the moat, not the architecture.

## Phi-3 vs same-tier peers (Q2 2024)

| Model | Date | Params | MMLU | HumanEval | On-device? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mistral 7B | 2023-09 | 7B | 60.1% | 30.5% | yes (4-bit quant) |
| Llama 2 7B | 2023-07 | 7B | 45.3% | 12.8% | yes |
| Gemma 7B | 2024-02 | 7B | 64.3% | 32.3% | yes |
| **Phi-3 Mini** | **2024-04** | **3.8B** | **68.8%** | **59.1%** | **yes (smartphone)** |
| Llama 3 8B | 2024-04 | 8B | 66.6% | 62.2% | yes |
| Phi-3 Medium | 2024-04 | 14B | 78.0% | 62.2% | yes (desktop) |

Phi-3 Mini's MMLU at 3.8B exceeds Llama 3 8B at half the parameters — the "data quality" thesis at its strongest single-data-point.

## Lineage

| Generation | Date | Innovation |
|---|---|---|
| Phi-1 / 1.5 | 2023-Jun/Sep | initial "textbooks are all you need" experiments |
| Phi-2 | 2023-12 | 2.7B model competitive with 7B Mistral |
| **Phi-3** | **2024-04** | **scale to 3.8B / 7B / 14B with strong results** |
| Phi-3.5 | 2024-08 | refresh; Mini-Instruct, MoE 16x3.8B variants |
| Phi-4 | 2024-12 | 14B; further reasoning emphasis |

nodes.title: DeepSeek-V2 — Open MoE with Multi-Head Latent Attention

model_spec.architecture: MoE + Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA) — KV cache compression via low-rank joint projection

benchmarks.name: API cost / 1M output
benchmarks.vs_baseline: GPT-4 Turbo $30 (107× cheaper), Claude Sonnet $15 (54× cheaper)

public_view.plain_english:
A Chinese AI lab's 236B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model,
released open-weight with a new attention architecture (MLA) that
dramatically reduced inference memory. Its API was priced at
$0.28 per million output tokens — two orders of magnitude cheaper
than GPT-4 Turbo. The pricing itself was the news: DeepSeek had
broken the perceived floor of frontier-quality LLM inference
pricing.
public_view.analogy:
Where Llama made open-weight LLMs practical, DeepSeek V2 made
open-weight frontier-quality LLMs *cheap* — priced at levels that
made every closed-model API vendor recalibrate their margins.
public_view.investment_angle:
DeepSeek V2's API pricing kicked off the Chinese LLM price war of
summer 2024 — Alibaba, Baidu, Tencent, ByteDance all cut prices
by 90%+ in the subsequent 60 days. More structurally, V2 proved
that "efficient MoE + aggressive inference optimization" could be
the moat, not just parameter count. The Multi-Head Latent
Attention innovation alone reduced KV cache by ~93% vs dense
attention, enabling the low pricing. Set up R1's January 2025
market-shaking release on the same architectural foundation.
public_view.why_it_matters:
DeepSeek V2 is the proximal cause of the global LLM inference
price collapse in 2024-25. Every frontier lab now has to explain
why they charge what they do — when a Chinese open model runs at
$0.28/1M tokens and meets 90% of enterprise quality needs.

citations.title: DeepSeek-V2: A Strong, Economical, and Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Language Model

nodes.body_md:
## What it is

A 236B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts decoder-only transformer with 21B active parameters per forward pass, released open-weight on May 6, 2024. Introduced two novel architectural components:

1. **Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA)** — instead of caching full K and V matrices per head, project them through a low-rank joint bottleneck before caching, then expand at attention time. Reduces KV cache memory by ~93% with negligible quality loss. This is the feature that enables the pricing.

2. **DeepSeekMoE** — finer-grained expert routing (160 routed experts + 2 shared experts, top-6 routing) vs Mixtral's 8-expert top-2. Research claim: fine-grained experts specialize more effectively, enabling better performance per active parameter.

## DeepSeek V2 vs 2024 mid-year peers

| Model | Date | Org | Active params | MMLU | HumanEval | API $ / 1M out |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4 Turbo | 2023-11 | OpenAI | undisclosed | 86.0% | ~85% | $30 |
| Claude 3 Opus | 2024-03 | Anthropic | undisclosed | 86.8% | 84.9% | $75 |
| Llama 3 70B | 2024-04 | Meta | 70B | 82.0% | 81.7% | ~$0.90 (Together) |
| GPT-4o | 2024-05 | OpenAI | undisclosed | 88.7% | 90.2% | $15 |
| **DeepSeek V2** | **2024-05** | **DeepSeek** | **21B** | **78.5%** | **81.1%** | **$0.28** |
| Qwen 2 72B | 2024-06 | Alibaba | 72B | 77.4% | 86.0% | ~$0.90 |

The economic frame: DeepSeek V2 delivered Llama-3-70B-class quality at ~⅓ the active-parameter inference cost (21B vs 70B), priced at ~⅓ of Together's Llama 3 hosting. End-to-end, ~9× cheaper than hosted Llama; ~100× cheaper than GPT-4 Turbo.

## What came next in the DeepSeek line

| Model | Date | Change |
|---|---|---|
| DeepSeek V2.5 | 2024-09 | improved coding + reasoning, 236B/21B |
| **DeepSeek V3** | **2024-12-26** | **671B/37B MoE, auxiliary-loss-free routing, $5.5M training cost** |
| DeepSeek R1 | 2025-01 | RL-only reasoning on V3 base |
| DeepSeek R1.5 | 2025-Q2 | refresh, tool-use integration |

V2 was the architectural foundation; V3 was the scale-up; R1 was the reasoning payoff. Each built directly on the prior generation's innovations.

nodes.title: AlphaFold 3 — All-Atom Biomolecular Prediction

model_spec.architecture: Diffusion-based all-atom structure prediction

public_view.plain_english:
DeepMind + Isomorphic Labs' May 2024 release — extends AlphaFold 2
from "predict the shape of one protein" to "predict the shape of
proteins together with DNA, RNA, drugs, and ions." This is what
you actually need for drug discovery, not just academic structural
biology. Released open for academic use through the AlphaFold
Server.
public_view.analogy:
AlphaFold 2 was a sculpture-of-the-protein generator. AlphaFold 3
is a sculpture-of-the-protein-with-drugs-bound-to-it generator —
the one drug discovery actually wanted.
public_view.investment_angle:
AlphaFold 3 is the centerpiece of Isomorphic Labs' drug-discovery
pipeline (DeepMind spinoff valued at ~$3B). Combined with
AlphaProteo (binder design) and AlphaMissense (variant effect),
DeepMind's biology stack is the leading AI-discovery platform.
Multiple pharmas have signed multi-year deals.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Made AI-driven structural biology actually useful for drug
discovery, not just basic science. Extended the reach of
AlphaFold's economic impact from "PDB completion" to "real
therapeutic pipelines." The 2024 chemistry Nobel went to David
Baker + Demis Hassabis + John Jumper for this lineage.

citations.title: Accurate structure prediction of biomolecular interactions with AlphaFold 3

nodes.body_md:
## What's new

| Capability | AlphaFold 2 | AlphaFold 3 |
|---|---|---|
| Single proteins | ✅ | ✅ |
| Protein-protein complexes | ✅ (multimer) | ✅ |
| Protein-DNA / RNA | ❌ | ✅ |
| Protein-ligand | ❌ | ✅ |
| Protein-ion | ❌ | ✅ |
| Diffusion architecture | ❌ (Evoformer) | ✅ |

## The DeepMind biology stack (2024-2026)

- AlphaFold 3 — structure prediction
- AlphaProteo — binder design
- AlphaMissense — variant effect prediction
- AlphaGenome — 1Mb-context DNA variant effects (June 2025)
- AlphaProof — math reasoning (also Nature, Nov 2025)

nodes.title: GPT-4o — Natively Multimodal OpenAI Frontier

model_spec.architecture: Single end-to-end model (text + audio + vision)

public_view.plain_english:
OpenAI's successor to GPT-4, with a single unified model handling
text, audio, and images natively — instead of separate models
stitched together. The "omni" of the name refers to this
multimodal integration. Launched with a real-time voice mode
designed to mimic natural conversation, complete with interruption
handling and emotional tone. Made flagship-tier capability free
to consumer ChatGPT users for the first time.
public_view.analogy:
Earlier multimodal AI was a translator relay — text goes to a text
model, audio to a speech model, image to a vision model, and a
coordinator passes messages. GPT-4o collapses all three into one
native speaker.

applications.product: Free tier ChatGPT (GPT-4o rolled out to all users)

public_view.investment_angle:
GPT-4o made flagship LLM access free — a strategic move against
Gemini (free on google.com) and Claude (free tier with daily caps).
The free tier drove ChatGPT to ~200M WAU by late 2024 and
reinforced OpenAI's consumer moat. Voice mode seeded the "AI as
ambient interface" category that Apple Intelligence (also built
partly on GPT-4o) then distributed to ~1.5B iOS devices. The
GPT-4o → GPT-4o mini split in July 2024 established the new
tier-pricing floor: flagship capability at Haiku-tier prices
($0.15 per 1M input tokens).
public_view.why_it_matters:
GPT-4o ended the era when "using the best AI" meant paying $20/mo.
Once frontier capability became free with an email address, the
user population expanded by an order of magnitude — reshaping
regulatory scrutiny, labor-market debate, and education policy
everywhere.

nodes.body_md:
## What it is

A frontier-tier model released May 13, 2024, trained end-to-end on a tokenizer that handles text, audio, and image inputs in the same representation space. Unlike GPT-4 (which used separate speech-to-text and text-to-speech models cascaded around a text LLM), GPT-4o's audio path is native — audio tokens in, audio tokens out.

The 320ms median speech-to-speech latency was the practical headline: for the first time, real-time voice conversation with an LLM felt like a phone call, not like a speech recognizer talking to a chatbot.

## Key spec deltas vs GPT-4 and peers

| Model | Date | Text MMLU | HumanEval | Audio | Vision | Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4 | 2023-03 | 86.4% | 67.0% | cascaded | GPT-4V (bolt-on) | 8k/32k |
| GPT-4 Turbo | 2023-11 | — | ~85% | cascaded | native | 128k |
| Claude 3 Opus | 2024-03 | 86.8% | 84.9% | text-only | native | 200k |
| Gemini 1.5 Pro | 2024-02 | 83.7% | 71.9% | native | native | 1M |
| **GPT-4o** | **2024-05** | **88.7%** | **90.2%** | **native (~320ms)** | **native** | **128k** |
| GPT-4o mini | 2024-07 | 82.0% | 87.2% | native | native | 128k |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet | 2024-06 | 88.7% | 92.0% | text-only | native | 200k |

GPT-4o was the frontier for ~3 weeks until Claude 3.5 Sonnet (2024-06-20) matched or exceeded it on most text and code benchmarks. The voice and free-tier distribution were the durable competitive moves; the benchmark lead was transient.

## The GPT-4o mini pricing reset

July 18, 2024: OpenAI launched GPT-4o mini at $0.15 / $0.60 per million input/output tokens — ~60% cheaper than Claude Haiku and cheaper than Llama 3 70B hosted at most inference platforms. Performance: MMLU 82.0%, HumanEval 87.2% — i.e., GPT-4 quality from 15 months earlier at 1/200th the price.

This was the single largest LLM price cut of 2024 and forced the entire tier to reprice within weeks (Claude Haiku → $0.25/$1.25, Gemini Flash → $0.075/$0.30). The floor of LLM inference pricing is now limited by inference compute cost, not by margin.

nodes.title: DeepSeek-Coder V2 — Open Coding Frontier

model_spec.parameters: 236B total / 21B active MoE; 16B and 6.7B sibling sizes
model_spec.architecture: DeepSeek-V2 MoE with code-specialized post-training

public_view.plain_english:
DeepSeek's June 2024 coding model — 236B mixture-of-experts
activating 21B per token, fine-tuned for code in 338 programming
languages. At release, it beat Codestral 22B and matched closed-
source GPT-4 Turbo on HumanEval. Open under a custom permissive
license. The Coder V3 / V3.2 successors keep DeepSeek at the
open coding frontier.
public_view.analogy:
DeepSeek-V2 is the general model that learned medicine, law,
history. Coder V2 is the same brain that went to coding boot camp
and stayed there.
public_view.investment_angle:
DeepSeek-Coder V2 made open-weight coding competitive with closed
Copilot at the frontier. Combined with V3.2's coding mode and
DeepSeekMath-V2, DeepSeek owns the "open coding leader" position
that GitHub Copilot's pricing model can't easily defend against.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Pushed open-weight coding models past GPT-4 Turbo. By late 2024,
serious enterprise coding deployments started defaulting to
DeepSeek-Coder for self-hosted Copilot replacements.

citations.title: DeepSeek-Coder-V2: Breaking the Barrier of Closed-Source Models in Code Intelligence

nodes.body_md:
## What's distinctive

- **338 languages** — broadest language coverage in any open coding model
- **236B / 21B active MoE** — efficient inference relative to parameter count
- **128K context** — fits most repos

nodes.title: Claude 3.5 Sonnet — Mid-Tier Surpasses Prior Flagships

model_spec.architecture: undisclosed; native multimodal (text + vision)

public_view.plain_english:
A mid-tier Claude 3.5 model that, on release, beat the previous
flagship (Claude 3 Opus) on most benchmarks while charging 1/5
the price. Cemented Anthropic's reputation as the leading model
for code generation — the dominant Claude model in IDEs like Cursor
and Windsurf throughout 2024-25.
public_view.analogy:
The mid-tier sedan that outperforms last year's flagship at half
the price — an industry pattern that reset everyone's expectations
for how often mid-tier was supposed to leap.

applications.product: Claude.ai (default for Pro and free tier)
Windsurf, Cline, Aider, GitHub Copilot Workspace

public_view.investment_angle:
Claude 3.5 Sonnet was the model that captured the developer market
from OpenAI. By late 2024, Anthropic's API revenue split had shifted
notably toward enterprise developer tools — Cursor's $9B valuation
(April 2025) is a direct downstream effect. The introduction of
"artifacts" (interactive code/UI rendered alongside chat) defined
a new product UX that competitors copied within months. The 3.5
nomenclature itself (vs incrementing to "Claude 4") signaled
Anthropic's intent to ship faster cadence than version-major
bumps suggested.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Claude 3.5 Sonnet established that "mid-tier > prior flagship" at
cheaper pricing was the new release cadence — meaning customers
should expect 6-month obsolescence of the model they're building
on. This reframed enterprise procurement: build on the model
abstraction (provider, not version) and assume continuous quality
upgrade.

nodes.body_md:
## What it is

A mid-tier model in the Claude 3.5 generation. At launch (June 20, 2024), the only model in the 3.5 line; Anthropic added Claude 3.5 Haiku (October 2024) but never released a 3.5 Opus, instead jumping to Claude 3.7 Sonnet and eventually Claude 4 Opus.

Quality position: matched or beat both GPT-4o and Claude 3 Opus on most reasoning, math, and coding benchmarks while priced identically to Claude 3 Sonnet ($3/$15 per 1M tokens — i.e., ⅕ the cost of Opus).

## How it landed in the competitive matrix (mid-2024)

| Model | Date | MMLU | HumanEval | GPQA | MATH | Price ($/1M out) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4 Turbo | 2023-11 | — | ~85% | 35.7 | — | $30 |
| Claude 3 Opus | 2024-03 | 86.8% | 84.9% | 50.4 | 60.1 | $75 |
| GPT-4o | 2024-05 | 88.7% | 90.2% | 53.6 | 76.6 | $15 |
| **Claude 3.5 Sonnet** | **2024-06** | **88.7%** | **92.0%** | **59.4** | **71.1** | **$15** |
| Llama 3.1 405B | 2024-07 | 88.6% | 89.0% | 51.1 | 73.8 | ~$5 (Together) |
| GPT-4o (Aug update) | 2024-08 | — | — | — | — | $15 |
| o1-preview | 2024-09 | 92.3% | 92.4% | 78.0 | 94.8 | $60 |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet (new) | 2024-10 | — | 93.7% | 65.0 | 78.3 | $15 |

Claude 3.5 Sonnet held the "best model overall, in many users' practical experience" position from June 2024 until at least Q1 2025 — substantially longer than the typical SOTA tenure.

## Artifacts — a UX shipping co-product

Released alongside the model: the "artifacts" feature in claude.ai split the conversation into a chat panel (left) and a rendered output panel (right) that automatically displays generated code, UI mockups, SVG diagrams, or documents. This UX pattern was almost immediately copied by ChatGPT (Canvas, October 2024), Gemini (Canvas, late 2024), and several agentic IDEs.

The product lesson: model quality alone wasn't enough to win developer mindshare; the way model output was surfaced to the user mattered just as much. Anthropic's product velocity in 2024-25 — artifacts, projects, computer use, MCP — is a key part of the Claude commercial story not captured by benchmark deltas.

nodes.title: ESM-3 — Generative Protein Language Model

model_spec.architecture: Multi-track protein language model (sequence + structure + function)

public_view.plain_english:
EvolutionaryScale (Meta spinoff) released ESM-3 in June 2024 — a
98-billion-parameter protein language model that handles sequence,
structure, and function in a unified framework. Unlike AlphaFold,
ESM-3 is generative — it can DESIGN new proteins, not just
predict the shape of existing ones.
public_view.analogy:
AlphaFold reads existing proteins. ESM-3 writes new ones.
Different jobs, both essential to AI-driven biology.
public_view.investment_angle:
EvolutionaryScale spun out of Meta in 2024 with $142M+ in
funding from Lux Capital and Nat Friedman. ESM-3 + ESMFold is
the open-source protein backbone that competes with closed
AlphaFold-based pipelines. Open weights matter for biotech
workflows that don't fit Google/DeepMind's licensing.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Made high-quality generative protein design open. Combined with
Baker Lab's RFdiffusion + ProteinMPNN, established the open-
source protein-engineering toolkit that competes with closed
Google offerings.

citations.title: Simulating 500 million years of evolution with a language model

nodes.body_md:
## ESM family

| Version | Year | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| ESM-1 | 2019 | First protein LM at scale |
| ESM-2 | 2022 | 15B params; ESMFold structure prediction |
| **ESM-3** | 2024 | 98B; multi-track sequence + structure + function |

nodes.title: Mistral Large 2 — France's Closed-Frontier Flagship

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer

public_view.plain_english:
Mistral's July 2024 flagship — a 123-billion-parameter dense model
for the European market. Released under the Mistral Research
License (free for research, paid for commercial). Followed by
Mistral Medium 3 and Mistral Small 4 as Mistral built a tiered
product line to compete with OpenAI's GPT-4o family.
public_view.analogy:
Mistral's answer to GPT-4 Turbo — same general capability tier,
European-hosted, with weights you can audit if you have a research
license. Less censored, more open about training data origins.
public_view.investment_angle:
Mistral is the European sovereign-AI bet. Mistral Large 2 anchored
the closed-API tier; combined with open Mixtral 8x22B and the 2025
Magistral reasoning models, Mistral covers a Llama + Claude
portfolio under EU jurisdiction. Strategic value to French and EU
digital-sovereignty buyers regardless of pure benchmark position.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Established that a non-US, non-Chinese lab could ship a frontier
model. Mistral Large 2 was Europe's first independent frontier
LLM and the foundation of the EU's AI sovereignty argument.

nodes.body_md:
## What it is

A 123B dense transformer with a 128K context window, native multilingual support across 11 languages, and strong code performance (92% HumanEval at release).

## Mistral product hierarchy

| Tier | Model | License |
|---|---|---|
| Flagship closed | Mistral Large 2 / Medium 3 / Small 4 | Research / Commercial |
| Open MoE | Mixtral 8x7B / 8x22B | Apache 2.0 |
| Open dense | Mistral 7B, Nemo 12B, Ministral 3B/8B | Apache 2.0 |
| Reasoning | Magistral | Apache 2.0 |
| Audio | Voxtral | Apache 2.0 |
| Multimodal | Pixtral 12B / Pixtral Large 124B | Apache 2.0 / Research |
| Code | Codestral 25.01 / Codestral Mamba / Devstral | Mixed |

nodes.title: AlphaProof — Math Olympiad Reasoning

model_spec.architecture: Gemini-based base model + RL fine-tuning + Lean 4 proof verification

benchmarks.name: International Mathematical Olympiad 2024
benchmarks.vs_baseline: first AI system at IMO medal-level performance

benchmarks.name: Days of self-RL training per problem

public_view.plain_english:
DeepMind's mathematical reasoning system that, in July 2024,
achieved silver-medal-level performance on the International
Mathematical Olympiad — solving 4 of 6 problems used in the
actual 2024 IMO competition. AlphaProof works by translating
natural-language problems into the Lean 4 theorem prover, then
using reinforcement learning to search for proofs that the
prover verifies. The verifiable-reward signal is what makes
the RL tractable.
public_view.analogy:
The "AlphaGo for math proofs" — same DeepMind playbook of
"neural network proposes, formal system verifies, RL trains."
The IMO milestone played the same cultural role for math as
AlphaGo's Lee Sedol match did for Go.
public_view.investment_angle:
AlphaProof is not directly monetized but its methodology
transferred into Gemini 2.5 Pro's "thinking mode" and into
the broader frontier-lab consensus that "verifiable rewards
+ RL" is the right recipe for reasoning capability. DeepSeek
R1 (Jan 2025) used essentially the same insight on math /
code domains. The IMO result also triggered a wave of
"automated theorem proving" startup interest — a niche that
had been moribund since the 1990s.
public_view.why_it_matters:
AlphaProof is the proof-of-concept that AI can reach
expert-human performance on rigorous mathematical reasoning
given the right scaffolding (formal verification). The
implications for mathematical research, formal verification
of software, and "AI as scientific collaborator" are still
being explored.

citations.title: AI achieves silver-medal standard solving International Mathematical Olympiad problems

nodes.body_md:
## What it is

A combined system from Google DeepMind for solving IMO-level mathematical problems. Two components:

1. **AlphaProof** — handles algebra and number theory problems via formal proof in Lean 4
2. **AlphaGeometry 2** — handles geometry problems via a different (geometry-specialized) formal system

For AlphaProof: a Gemini-derived language model translates natural-language problems into Lean 4 statements, then iteratively proposes proof attempts. Lean 4's type checker provides ground-truth verification: a proof either compiles or doesn't. RL training optimizes the LM against this verifiable reward.

The reasoning traces are extensive — solving a single IMO problem can take 1-3 days of compute (parallel search + RL fine-tuning per problem). This is "extreme inference-time compute" — much more than even o1-pro consumes per query.

## IMO 2024 results

| Problem | Type | AlphaProof / AlphaGeometry result | Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Algebra | ✓ correct proof | minutes |
| 2 | Algebra | ✓ correct proof | minutes |
| 3 | Combinatorics | ✗ unsolved | exceeded budget |
| 4 | Geometry (AlphaGeometry 2) | ✓ correct proof | seconds |
| 5 | Combinatorics | ✗ unsolved | exceeded budget |
| 6 | Algebra | ✓ correct proof (the hardest problem of the year) | days |

Total: 28/42 points — silver medal threshold (top ~150 of ~600 participating students). Gold medal threshold (top ~50) was 29 points, which AlphaProof missed by 1 point.

Notably: problem 6 was solved by AlphaProof but only ~5 of 600 human students did so. Problem 3 (combinatorics) defeated AlphaProof; combinatorics is typically the hardest area for formal-proof systems because the combinatorial search space is large.

## Lineage and influence

AlphaProof's technique — neural network proposes, formal system verifies, RL on the verified-correct examples — is essentially the same recipe DeepSeek R1 used 6 months later for general math/code reasoning (with code-execution / answer-checking as the verifier instead of Lean). The methodology transferred from "specialized formal-math system" to "general reasoning model" within roughly a year — one of the faster technique-transfer rates in the modern LLM era.

nodes.title: Perplexity Sonar — Search-Native Generation

model_spec.parameters: Sonar Small 8B / Sonar Large 70B / Sonar Huge undisclosed
model_spec.architecture: Llama 3.1 base + Perplexity post-training for citation-grounded answers + native web search integration

benchmarks.name: Citation accuracy (vs ungrounded LLM)
benchmarks.vs_baseline: raw GPT-4 ~50% (hallucination rate on factual queries)

benchmarks.name: Latency
benchmarks.score: <1s to first token incl. web search

public_view.plain_english:
Perplexity AI's family of LLM variants explicitly tuned for
"answer this question and cite your sources." Released as
paid API endpoints (Sonar Small, Large, Huge) in 2024. The
underlying technique — Llama 3.1 fine-tuned for citation
quality and integrated tightly with Perplexity's web search
backend — became the default architecture behind any
consumer "AI search" product.
public_view.analogy:
The "AI as Wikipedia of the moment" — same role search
engines played in the 2000s, restructured around generating
natural-language answers instead of retrieving page lists.
Sonar is the LLM optimized for this specific use case.

applications.product: Sonar API (Apple Maps, Snapchat, Carrot Weather, etc.)

public_view.investment_angle:
Perplexity raised $500M Series C at $9B valuation in
November 2024 — a valuation premised on AI search as a
standalone category large enough to coexist with Google
Search. By 2025-26, Perplexity's positioning had evolved
toward "agentic browser" (the Perplexity Comet browser
launch) — extending from "search" to "do tasks for me on
the web." Sonar remains the model layer underneath both
cases. Strategic risk: Google's own AI Overviews + Gemini
integration in Search may close the differentiation.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Perplexity Sonar represents the "search-grounded LLM" as a
distinct model category — a specialization that generic
LLMs (GPT, Claude) approach but don't own as a primary
product surface. Whether AI search remains a separate
category or gets absorbed by general-purpose chat is the
open question.

nodes.body_md:
## What it is

A family of LLM API endpoints from Perplexity AI optimized for grounded question-answering with citations. Three sizes:

| Variant | Base | Specialization |
|---|---|---|
| Sonar Small | Llama 3.1 8B | fast Q&A with web grounding |
| Sonar Large | Llama 3.1 70B | higher-quality answers |
| Sonar Huge | undisclosed (~Llama 3.1 405B class) | flagship, longest reasoning |

The post-training and serving infrastructure are Perplexity's contributions, not the base models themselves. The combined system (LLM + retrieval + citation post-processing) is what's productized.

## Perplexity vs concurrent "AI search" peers

| Product | Date | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Bing Chat / Copilot | 2023-02 | GPT-4 + Bing search |
| Google AI Overviews | 2024-05 | Gemini + Google Search; default-on |
| **Perplexity (consumer + Sonar API)** | **2022-12 / 2024-09** | **dedicated AI-search product + API** |
| You.com | 2022 | early entrant, struggled to differentiate |
| Phind | 2022 | dev-focused AI search |
| ChatGPT Search | 2024-10 | OpenAI's response — search inside ChatGPT |
| Claude Web Search | 2025-Q2 | Anthropic's response |

Perplexity's bet: search-as-primary-product can sustain a standalone company despite Google's distribution and OpenAI's brand. By 2026 the bet is partly working (revenue scaling, valuation holding) and partly under pressure (consumer AI-search has become table-stakes for every chat product).

nodes.title: OpenAI o1 — Inference-Time Reasoning

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer + RL on reasoning traces (specifics undisclosed)

public_view.plain_english:
OpenAI's first model trained explicitly to "think before it answers"
— generating a long chain of internal reasoning before producing
its final response. On math olympiad and competitive-programming
problems, it crossed thresholds (top human percentiles) that prior
models couldn't approach. Trade-off: each query takes 10-60
seconds and costs 5-50× more than GPT-4o.
public_view.analogy:
Earlier models answered like contestants on a quiz show — fast,
confident, sometimes wrong. o1 answered more like a graduate
student writing a problem set — slower, with visible scratch
work, and substantially more accurate on hard problems.
public_view.investment_angle:
o1 opened a new scaling axis — inference-time compute — that doesn't
require larger pretraining runs. Implications: (1) future capability
gains are partly decoupled from $1B+ training spend, shifting
competitive ground from "who can afford the next model" to "who
builds the best reasoning RL pipeline"; (2) inference-time scaling
favors compute-constrained labs (Anthropic, DeepMind) and
challenges hyperscaler advantages (OpenAI/Microsoft); (3) the
economics of "spend more compute for higher-quality answers"
creates new monetization tiers (ChatGPT Pro at $200/mo). Triggered
rapid follow-on releases of reasoning models from every frontier
lab through 2025.
public_view.why_it_matters:
o1 ended the "scaling laws determine everything" era. After o1,
capability could be bought at inference time, not just training
time. This is the most important technical pivot in the LLM
landscape since GPT-3.

citations.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models

nodes.body_md:
## What it is

A frontier model trained to internalize chain-of-thought reasoning. Architecturally a decoder-only transformer (specifics undisclosed), but post-trained with reinforcement learning on long-form reasoning traces. At inference, the model generates a hidden reasoning trace (often 1,000-10,000 tokens) before emitting a final answer; users see only the answer plus a short summary of the reasoning.

OpenAI declined to publish the reward signal, the RL algorithm, or the dataset of reasoning traces. The most-cited speculation: process supervision (PRM-style reward modeling, per the "Let's Verify Step by Step" paper from OpenAI in 2023), combined with verifiable-reward domains (math, code) where ground truth is checkable.

## Capability step-change in math and code

| Benchmark | GPT-4o | o1-preview | o1 | Human reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AIME 2024 | 13.4% | 56.7% | 83.3% | top 500 in US ~75% |
| Codeforces | 11th %ile | 62nd %ile | 89th %ile | — |
| GPQA Diamond | 53.6% | 73.3% | 78.0% | PhD expert ~70% |
| MATH | 76.6% | 85.5% | 94.8% | — |
| LiveCodeBench | 38.2% | 76.5% | 89.0% | — |
| MMLU | 88.7% | 90.8% | 92.3% | — |

The "uniform" gains on text-only benchmarks (MMLU) are modest. The differential gains on verifiable-reward benchmarks (math, code, science) are extreme. This pattern — reasoning models help where there's a checkable answer, much less where there isn't — has held across all subsequent reasoning-model releases.

## Successor reasoning models (the wave o1 triggered)

| Model | Date | Org | AIME | GPQA | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **o1** | **2024-09** | **OpenAI** | **83.3%** | **78.0%** | first |
| o1-pro | 2024-12 | OpenAI | 86% | 79% | even longer thinking |
| o3 | 2025-01 (preview) | OpenAI | 96.7% | 87.7% | full release April 2025 |
| Claude 3.7 Sonnet | 2025-02 | Anthropic | 80.0% | 84.8% | extended-thinking mode |
| DeepSeek R1 | 2025-01 | DeepSeek | 79.8% | 71.5% | open-weight |
| Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking | 2025-01 | Google | 73.3% | 74.2% | low-latency reasoning |
| Qwen QwQ-32B | 2024-11 | Alibaba | 50.0% | 65.2% | open-weight reasoning |

DeepSeek R1's January 2025 release — open-weight reasoning model approaching o1 quality — was the strategic shock of the year, briefly knocking ~$600B off Nvidia's market cap as investors questioned closed-model moats.

## The hidden reasoning trace controversy

OpenAI showed users only a "summary" of the reasoning trace, not the trace itself. Stated rationale: prevent competitive scraping for distillation training. Researcher critique: the hidden trace makes safety audits, jailbreak research, and capability benchmarking structurally harder. As of mid-2025, no frontier reasoning lab has reverted to fully visible reasoning traces.

nodes.title: Qwen 2.5 — Open Frontier-Comparable from Alibaba

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer + GQA + RoPE + SwiGLU

public_view.plain_english:
Alibaba's Qwen 2.5 generation, released September 2024, was the
first Chinese open-weight model family to exceed Llama 3.1 on
most benchmarks at comparable size. The release covered seven
sizes (0.5B → 72B) plus specialized variants for code (Coder)
and math, all under Apache 2.0. By late 2024, Qwen 2.5 had
overtaken Llama as the most-downloaded model family on Hugging
Face globally.
public_view.analogy:
The "Toyota strategy" of Chinese open LLMs — every market segment,
every quality tier, broad license, aggressive shipping cadence.
Where Llama is a sedan family, Qwen 2.5 is sedan + SUV + truck
+ pickup + scooter, all priced lower.

applications.product: Qwen-Coder default in many AI IDEs
Most-downloaded model family on Hugging Face by Q4 2024

public_view.investment_angle:
Qwen 2.5 was the pivot point where Chinese open-weight models
stopped being "good for the price" and became "best-in-class at
any price for the size." Combined with DeepSeek V2's pricing
revolution earlier in 2024, the Chinese open ecosystem effectively
set the global ceiling for what closed-model providers could
charge for non-frontier tasks. By end of 2024, Aliyun's reported
AI revenue was growing 3-4× YoY, much of it Qwen-driven inference.
The strategic pattern: don't try to beat OpenAI / Anthropic at
the absolute frontier; instead, dominate every tier just below
with open weights and aggressive cloud distribution.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Qwen 2.5 made it impossible for any serious enterprise procurement
decision in 2025 to skip the open-Chinese-model option. Even US
enterprises with concerns about Chinese-origin models routinely
benchmark against Qwen 2.5 / 3 to negotiate better pricing from
OpenAI / Anthropic. The market structural effect is large.

nodes.body_md:
## What it is

A multi-size open-weight foundation model family released September 19, 2024 with sizes 0.5B / 1.5B / 3B / 7B / 14B / 32B / 72B. All variants under Apache 2.0 license, trained on 18T tokens (the largest publicly-disclosed pretraining dataset for an open model at the time). Architectural inheritance from Qwen 2 with refinements; the headline gain came from data-quality work and the much larger token count.

## Specialized variants in the Qwen 2.5 family

| Variant | Sizes | Specialization |
|---|---|---|
| Qwen 2.5 (base) | 0.5B-72B | general purpose |
| Qwen 2.5-Instruct | 0.5B-72B | chat / instruction following |
| Qwen 2.5-Coder | 0.5B-32B | code generation; Coder-32B competitive with GPT-4o-mini on coding |
| Qwen 2.5-Math | 1.5B / 7B / 72B | math reasoning |
| Qwen 2.5-VL | 3B / 7B / 72B | multimodal vision |

The Coder and Math variants exemplify a productization pattern Western labs have largely avoided — domain-specific specialized model variants instead of one general flagship.

## Qwen 2.5-72B vs concurrent open peers (Q4 2024)

| Model | Date | Org | Params | MMLU | HumanEval | GSM8K | MATH |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Llama 3 70B | 2024-04 | Meta | 70B | 82.0% | 81.7% | 93.0% | 68.0% |
| Llama 3.1 70B | 2024-07 | Meta | 70B | 83.1% | 80.5% | 95.1% | 68.0% |
| Llama 3.1 405B | 2024-07 | Meta | 405B | 88.6% | 89.0% | 96.8% | 73.8% |
| **Qwen 2.5 72B** | **2024-09** | **Alibaba** | **72B** | **85.3%** | **86.6%** | **95.8%** | **83.1%** |
| Mistral Large 2 | 2024-07 | Mistral | 123B | 84.0% | 92.0% | 93.0% | 75.0% |
| DeepSeek V2.5 | 2024-09 | DeepSeek | 236B/21B | 80.4% | 89.0% | 89.3% | 74.7% |

Qwen 2.5-72B is the strongest dense open model at its size by a substantial margin in math and coding. The MATH benchmark in particular (+15 over Llama 3.1) was driven by Qwen 2.5-Math's training-data investment.

## The "AI tigers" funding context

By Qwen 2.5's release, Alibaba had positioned itself differently from China's "AI tiger" startups (Zhipu, Moonshot, Baichuan, MiniMax). Where the tigers raised hundreds of millions for narrow flagship products, Alibaba treated AI as infrastructure investment — open Qwen as ecosystem fuel, monetized through cloud (Aliyun GPU rental, model API, fine-tuning services) rather than direct model sales. This positioning turned out to be more sustainable as the AI tigers struggled with monetization in 2025.

nodes.title: Molmo — Allen AI's Open Vision-Language Model

model_spec.architecture: Vision encoder + Olmo / Qwen language backbone

public_view.plain_english:
Allen AI's open vision-language family — released September 2024
with sizes from 1B to 72B. Trained with a small (1M-image)
high-quality dataset (PixMo), Molmo demonstrated that you don't
need internet-scale image-text pretraining to get strong VLMs.
Its signature trick: "pointing" — the model outputs (x,y)
coordinates to point at things in images.
public_view.analogy:
A vision model that uses its finger. Most VLMs describe an image
in words; Molmo can also point at the specific pixel where the
answer is.
public_view.investment_angle:
Molmo's "pointing" output reframes VLMs as components of
embodied / interactive systems, not just chat. Used heavily in
open robotics research where the model needs to specify a
grasping point or attention region. The Molmo 2 / MolmoAct
extensions (with Ai2's robotics work) make this a real toolchain.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Showed that high-quality small datasets + open weights beat the
Llava-style "scrape and pretrain" approach for many VLM tasks.
Molmo 72B held its own against Gemini 1.5 Pro and Claude 3 Opus
at release with a fraction of the data.

citations.title: Molmo and PixMo: Open Weights and Open Data for State-of-the-Art Multimodal Models

nodes.body_md:
## Family

| Variant | Backbone | Size |
|---|---|---|
| MolmoE-1B | OLMoE | 1B active |
| Molmo-7B-O | OLMo 7B | 7B |
| Molmo-7B-D | Qwen2 7B | 7B |
| Molmo-72B | Qwen2 72B | 72B |
| **Molmo 2** (Dec 2025) | OLMo / Qwen 3 | Multi-frame video reasoning |

nodes.title: π₀ — Physical Intelligence's Open Robot Foundation

model_spec.architecture: VLA with flow-matching action head

public_view.plain_english:
Physical Intelligence's flagship vision-language-action model —
π₀ (October 2024) opened the open-weight VLA category. π₀.₅
(April 2025) added open-world generalization; π*0.6 (Nov 2025)
added experience-based RL. Released through the openpi GitHub
repo, with reference deployments on commodity robot arms.
public_view.analogy:
Pi is the Android of robot brains — open foundation that lab and
startup robots can fine-tune for their specific gripper /
workspace, instead of every team training from scratch.
public_view.investment_angle:
Physical Intelligence raised $400M+ at $2B+ valuation on the
open-weight robotics-foundation thesis. Pi sits one layer above
the hardware (which they also build) and one layer below
application (which their customers / partners build). The open
weights are a wedge to make Pi the default VLA stack.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Made open-weight VLA a real thing — before π₀, every serious VLA
was closed (Google's RT, Tesla's, Figure's). Pi proved you could
open-source the architecture and still build a business on
hardware + integration + commercial fine-tuning.

citations.title: π₀: A Vision-Language-Action Flow Model for General Robot Control

nodes.body_md:
## Pi family timeline

| Model | Date | What's new |
|---|---|---|
| **π₀** | Oct 2024 | First flow-matching VLA |
| **π₀-FAST** | Late 2024 | Latency-optimized variant |
| **π₀.₅** | Apr 2025 | Open-world generalization |
| **π*0.6** | Nov 2025 | Experience-based RL |

nodes.title: Hunyuan-Large — Tencent's First Open Frontier MoE

model_spec.architecture: Cross-Layer Attention (CLA) MoE

public_view.plain_english:
Tencent's first major open-weight large model — a 389-billion-
parameter mixture-of-experts that activates 52B per token. Released
Nov 2024, it brought Tencent into the same league as DeepSeek and
Alibaba on the open frontier.
public_view.analogy:
Mixtral with 50× the parameters. Same idea — only some "experts"
work on each input — at a scale only hyperscalers had matched
until then.
public_view.investment_angle:
Tencent's late but full entry into the open-weight LLM race.
Hunyuan-Large was the proof point that Tencent could match
DeepSeek / Alibaba at scale; the follow-on Hunyuan-T1, A13B, and
Hunyuan 2.0 (Dec 2025, 406B/32B-active) build on this base.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Made it three Chinese hyperscalers (Alibaba, DeepSeek/Tencent)
shipping frontier-scale open MoE — a market structure unique to
China through 2024–2026.

citations.title: Hunyuan-Large: An Open-Source MoE Model with 52 Billion Activated Parameters

nodes.body_md:
## What it shipped

- 389B total / 52B active MoE — at release, the largest open MoE
- Cross-Layer Attention (CLA) for KV-cache compression
- 256K context, training on 7T+ multilingual tokens
- Followed by Hunyuan-Turbo (proprietary), Hunyuan-T1 (reasoning, March 2025), Hunyuan-A13B open MoE (June 2025), and Hunyuan 2.0 (406B/32B active, Dec 2025)

nodes.title: Pixtral Large — Mistral's 124B Multimodal Frontier

model_spec.architecture: Mistral Large 2 + dedicated vision encoder

public_view.plain_english:
Mistral's frontier-tier multimodal model — Mistral Large 2 with a
dedicated 1B vision encoder. At launch (Nov 2024), it led MathVista
(visual math reasoning) and matched closed-source frontier on most
visual tasks. Open-weight under the Mistral Research License.
public_view.analogy:
Pixtral is the visual brain bolted onto Mistral's text reasoner.
A specialized eye that feeds detailed observations into a large
general-purpose mind.
public_view.investment_angle:
Vision capabilities have become table stakes for serious LLM
products. Pixtral Large gave Mistral a credible vision answer to
GPT-4o and Claude 3.5; the smaller Pixtral 12B (Apache 2.0) is a
favorite for self-hosted RAG-with-images workflows.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Showed that visual reasoning (chart understanding, diagram parsing,
document QA) could beat closed frontier models on specific tasks
while shipping open weights — particularly for math-heavy visual
benchmarks.

nodes.body_md:
## What it shipped

- 124B total params (123B Mistral Large 2 base + 1B vision encoder)
- Native variable-resolution image input
- Pixtral 12B sibling under Apache 2.0 — smaller, popular for self-hosted vision RAG
- Followed by Mistral's broader push into multimodal across Voxtral (audio) and Magistral (reasoning)

nodes.title: Model Context Protocol — Open Tool / Context Standard

benchmarks.name: Servers / integrations published (community)
benchmarks.vs_baseline: first protocol with substantial multi-vendor adoption in agentic AI

public_view.plain_english:
An open protocol Anthropic released in November 2024 for
standardizing how AI agents talk to external tools, data
sources, and contextual services. Instead of every AI app
integrating every tool individually (M apps × N tools = M×N
integrations), MCP lets any tool publish an MCP server once
and any AI app consume it. Quickly adopted by OpenAI, Google,
cursor / IDE vendors, and thousands of community
integrations.
public_view.analogy:
The "USB for AI" analogy is the one Anthropic used in the
launch post. Before USB, every peripheral had its own
cable / driver / connector; after USB, one standard worked
across the industry. MCP plays the same role for the
AI-to-tool integration layer.

applications.product: Thousands of community MCP servers (Slack, Linear, GitHub, databases, etc.)

public_view.investment_angle:
MCP's adoption trajectory was the surprise of late-2024 / 2025
AI infrastructure. By accepting an Anthropic-originated
standard, OpenAI and Google effectively conceded the
protocol-design layer — choosing interop over dominance.
Strategically: if AI agents become the dominant
productivity-software interface, the protocol layer
underneath is comparable in importance to HTTP for the web.
Anthropic's positioning as the protocol's originator gives
it gravitational pull in the agent-developer ecosystem
even when other models match Claude's capabilities.
Several companies now build "MCP server marketplace"
businesses (similar to npm registries), and MCP-related
consulting / integration is a growing services category.
public_view.why_it_matters:
MCP is the most successful open standard to come out of
the LLM era. Whether it persists as the dominant agent
protocol over the next 5 years (vs being replaced by
something else) is the open question — but its current
momentum makes it the de facto standard for agent
integration in 2025-26.

nodes.title: OLMo 2 — Allen AI's Fully Open Foundation

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer

public_view.plain_english:
The Allen Institute's truly-open language model — not just weights,
but the training data (Dolma), the training code, intermediate
checkpoints, and evaluation suites. OLMo 2 (and the instruction-
tuned Tulu 3 family) is what most academic ML researchers use when
they need a model they can REPRODUCE, not just download.
public_view.analogy:
Llama is "we'll show you the cake." OLMo is "here's the cake, the
recipe, every ingredient with sourcing, and a video of us baking
it." The full reproducibility difference matters for science even
when product capability is similar.
public_view.investment_angle:
OLMo's economic impact is in the academic + government tier where
full reproducibility is required. Doesn't compete head-to-head
with Llama or Qwen for product, but defines the upper bound on
"open enough for science." Used heavily by safety / interpretability
research labs (Anthropic interp team, Apollo Research, etc.).
public_view.why_it_matters:
Pushes the definition of "open source AI" — exposes how partial
Llama, Mistral, and Qwen open releases really are. Ai2's continued
pressure (Tulu 3 405B, Molmo, OLMoE) anchors the open-science end
of the model spectrum.

nodes.body_md:
## What "fully open" means

| Layer | Llama 3 | Qwen 3 | OLMo 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Final weights | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Training code | partial | partial | ✅ |
| Training data | ❌ | partial | ✅ (Dolma) |
| Intermediate checkpoints | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Evaluation harness | ❌ | partial | ✅ |
| Apache / MIT license | ❌ (custom) | mostly | ✅ Apache |

The OLMo / Tulu / Molmo / OLMoE stack is the only complete open-source pipeline at modern frontier scale.

nodes.title: QwQ-32B Preview — Alibaba's Open Reasoning Model

model_spec.architecture: Qwen 2.5-32B base + RL on reasoning traces

public_view.plain_english:
Alibaba's first open-weight reasoning model, released November
2024 as a "preview." Used the same RL-on-reasoning-traces recipe
OpenAI used for o1, but with the model weights downloadable —
making it the first time anyone outside the frontier US labs
could study how reasoning capability is trained in. Its release
8 weeks before DeepSeek R1 partly set the expectation that
"open reasoning" would arrive within months, not years.
public_view.analogy:
The early-access preview that proved the recipe worked outside
OpenAI. Like Linux's early-90s kernel — not the final article,
but the demonstration that ended the "this can only be done
inside one company" framing.
public_view.investment_angle:
QwQ-32B's release signaled that open-weight reasoning was no
longer a research aspiration but a near-term commercial reality.
Combined with DeepSeek R1's January 2025 release, it made clear
that closed reasoning models (o1, o1-pro) would face open
competition within the same generation, not the next. This
structurally limits the pricing power of o-series APIs and
explains why OpenAI accelerated o3 disclosure timing in early
2025.
public_view.why_it_matters:
QwQ proved that the o1 recipe was reproducible from public
research alone — no OpenAI insider knowledge required. That
proof, more than the model's specific capabilities, was the
contribution. By Q1 2025, ten different labs had released
reasoning models; the technique had become a shared methodology.

citations.title: QwQ: Reflect Deeply on the Boundaries of the Unknown

nodes.body_md:
## What it is

A 32-billion-parameter reasoning model released November 28, 2024 as "QwQ-32B-Preview" by Alibaba's Qwen team. Built on the Qwen 2.5-32B base with RL post-training on reasoning traces (similar in spirit to OpenAI's o1 recipe, though Alibaba did not disclose detailed training specifics). At launch, the strongest open-weight model on math benchmarks (AIME, MATH-500) by a substantial margin.

## QwQ-32B-Preview vs o1-preview vs alternatives

| Model | Date | Org | Params | AIME | MATH-500 | GPQA Diamond | Open? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4o | 2024-05 | OpenAI | undisclosed | 13.4% | 76.6% | 53.6% | API |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet | 2024-06 | Anthropic | undisclosed | 16.0% | 71.1% | 59.4% | API |
| o1-preview | 2024-09 | OpenAI | undisclosed | 56.7% | 85.5% | 73.3% | API |
| o1 | 2024-09 | OpenAI | undisclosed | 83.3% | 94.8% | 78.0% | API |
| **QwQ-32B-Preview** | **2024-11** | **Alibaba** | **32B** | **50.0%** | **90.6%** | **65.2%** | **open** |
| DeepSeek R1 | 2025-01 | DeepSeek | 671B/37B | 79.8% | 97.3% | 71.5% | open |
| QwQ-32B (final) | 2025-03 | Alibaba | 32B | 79.5% | — | — | open |

QwQ-32B-Preview was the first open model to clearly exceed GPT-4o-class non-reasoning models on math, demonstrating that the reasoning RL recipe added meaningful capability that scaling alone hadn't.

## What QwQ contributed methodologically

While the formal training recipe was not published, the release showed:

- A 32B base model could host the reasoning capability — no need for GPT-4-class compute.
- RL on verifiable-reward problems (math, code) generalized somewhat to non-verifiable reasoning (the GPQA improvement, though smaller, was real).
- The "extended thinking" outputs were highly verbose (often 5,000-15,000 tokens for one response) — the inference-time cost trade-off was real.

These findings preview what DeepSeek R1's much more detailed training disclosure would document in January 2025.

nodes.title: Phi-4 — Microsoft's Synthetic-Data Small Model

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer; trained heavily on synthetic data

public_view.plain_english:
Microsoft's December 2024 small model — only 14 billion parameters
but matching GPT-4o on reasoning benchmarks. The trick: Phi-4 was
trained mostly on SYNTHETIC data — examples generated by a larger
model and filtered for quality. It's a proof point that data
quality matters more than raw web-scraped quantity.
public_view.analogy:
Most LLMs read the entire internet (a noisy diet). Phi-4 was raised
on a curated diet of textbook-quality examples generated to cover
exactly the skills it would be tested on.
public_view.investment_angle:
Phi-4 changed the procurement math for SLM (small language model)
deployments — 14B running on a single GPU at GPT-4o-class quality
reshaped on-prem and edge AI. Microsoft uses it as the anchor for
cheap inference in Azure and Copilot-cheap deployments. Followed
by Phi-4-reasoning-15B (March 2026) hitting frontier reasoning on
a single GPU.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Validated synthetic-data training at scale. Showed that closed
labs' web-scraping advantage matters less than once thought —
a small model + great data can beat a huge model + average data.

nodes.body_md:
## What's distinctive

- **14B dense** — small enough to fit on a single A100 in bf16
- **MIT license** — fully permissive for commercial use
- **Synthetic data heavy** — majority of training tokens are LLM-generated and filtered
- **Reasoning focus** — Phi-4-reasoning and Phi-4-reasoning-plus extend with o1-style chain-of-thought training

nodes.title: DeepSeek V3 — 671B Open MoE at $5.5M Training Cost

model_spec.architecture: MoE (256 routed + 1 shared expert, top-8) + MLA + FP8 mixed-precision training + auxiliary-loss-free load balancing

public_view.plain_english:
DeepSeek's December 2024 frontier release: a 671-billion-parameter
Mixture-of-Experts model approaching GPT-4o quality, with weights
open and a published training cost of $5.5M — one to two orders
of magnitude below US frontier estimates. Quietly released on
December 26, 2024, it would become the foundation for R1 four
weeks later and the source of the January 2025 Nvidia stock shock.
public_view.analogy:
Where Llama 3 brought open weights to GPT-4 territory at $20B+
of capex, DeepSeek V3 brought open weights to GPT-4o territory
at a publicly-reported price an undergraduate research budget
could (in principle) have funded. Whether the $5.5M number holds
up to scrutiny matters less than the fact that nobody could
refute it conclusively.
public_view.analogy_lite:
The first open frontier-class model trained for less than the
cost of a Manhattan condo.

applications.product: Hosted at every major inference platform within 2 weeks
applications.company: Together, Fireworks, Groq, Hyperbolic, Novita, etc.

public_view.investment_angle:
DeepSeek V3 set up the R1 release that crashed Nvidia's stock by
$600B on January 27, 2025. The investment-thesis attack: if a
Chinese lab can reach GPT-4o quality for $5.5M of training
compute, the assumption that frontier capability requires
$1B+ training runs (and therefore Nvidia GPU sales of similar
magnitude) breaks down. Subsequent independent estimates of
DeepSeek's actual fully-loaded compute spend ranged $50M-$1.6B
(including research, ablations, infrastructure). The fact that
no one could conclusively refute the $5.5M number — and that
even the higher estimates were 10-20× below US frontier
spend — was the structural attack on the AI capex thesis.
public_view.why_it_matters:
DeepSeek V3, not R1, is the model that actually changed the
economics. R1 added the reasoning headlines, but V3 was where
the cost shock originated. Every subsequent debate about US
export controls, AI capex sustainability, and Chinese AI
capability has V3 as a central data point.

nodes.body_md:
## What it is

A 671-billion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts decoder-only transformer with 37B active parameters per forward pass, released open-weight on December 26, 2024 with a detailed technical report. Three architectural innovations beyond DeepSeek V2:

1. **Auxiliary-loss-free load balancing** — replaces the standard MoE auxiliary loss (which can degrade quality) with a bias-term-based routing scheme. Empirically reduces routing instability without quality cost.

2. **Multi-Token Prediction (MTP)** — predict the next 2 tokens during training as an auxiliary objective, enabling speculative decoding at inference for ~85% acceptance rates and ~1.8× decoding speedup.

3. **FP8 mixed-precision training** — first frontier-scale open release trained natively in FP8 (with critical-path FP32 fallback). Cut memory bandwidth requirements significantly, contributing to the headline cost number.

## Training cost claim

Per DeepSeek's technical report, full training cost decomposed as:

| Stage | GPU-hours (H800) | Cost @ $2/hr |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-training | 2.664M | $5.328M |
| Context extension | 0.119M | $0.238M |
| Post-training | 0.005M | $0.010M |
| **Total** | **2.788M** | **$5.576M** |

This excludes "the costs associated with prior research and ablation experiments on architectures, algorithms, or data" — a substantial caveat. Independent estimates of the fully-loaded cost (including research compute, infrastructure amortization, salaries) ranged $50M-$1.6B. The reported number is the marginal compute cost of the final training run only.

## DeepSeek V3 vs frontier peers (Q4 2024 / Q1 2025)

| Model | Date | Org | Active params | MMLU | HumanEval | MATH-500 | GPQA | API $ / 1M out |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4o (Aug update) | 2024-08 | OpenAI | undisclosed | 88.7% | 90.2% | 76.6% | 53.6% | $15 |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet (new) | 2024-10 | Anthropic | undisclosed | 88.7% | 93.7% | 78.3% | 65.0% | $15 |
| Llama 3.1 405B | 2024-07 | Meta | 405B | 88.6% | 89.0% | 73.8% | 51.1% | ~$5 (Together) |
| Qwen 2.5 72B | 2024-09 | Alibaba | 72B | 85.3% | 86.6% | 83.1% | 49.0% | ~$0.90 |
| **DeepSeek V3** | **2024-12** | **DeepSeek** | **37B** | **88.5%** | **82.6%** | **90.2%** | **59.1%** | **$1.10 / $0.27 cached** |
| o1 | 2024-09 | OpenAI | undisclosed | 92.3% | 92.4% | 94.8% | 78.0% | $60 |

V3 hit GPT-4o-class on MMLU, exceeded GPT-4o on math, was somewhat behind on coding. The pricing — $1.10 per million output tokens, with caching pushing it to $0.27 — was 14-55× below GPT-4o's API.

## What V3 led to

- **DeepSeek R1** (2025-01-20): RL post-training on V3 for reasoning capability; the version that triggered the global AI policy and market response.
- **DeepSeek V3.5 / V4** (2025): incremental refinements following the same playbook.
- **Industry follow-on**: every major open-weight lab (Qwen, Mistral, Llama 4) shifted toward MoE-default architectures in 2025; auxiliary-loss-free routing became standard.

V3 is the architectural ancestor of essentially all serious open-weight 2025 frontier work.

nodes.title: Codestral 25.01 — Mistral's Coding Model

model_spec.architecture: Decoder-only transformer; coding fine-tune

public_view.plain_english:
Mistral's January 2025 coding model — 22B parameters, 256K
context, 80+ programming languages. Codestral excels at
fill-in-the-middle (predicting code in the middle of a function,
not just appending). The Codestral Mamba sibling uses
Mamba state-space architecture for very long context, and
Devstral extends to agentic workflows.
public_view.analogy:
The European answer to GitHub Copilot — same use case (autocomplete
+ chat coding), open-weight, hosted in EU.
public_view.investment_angle:
Codestral is Mistral's commercial-coding play targeting
enterprises that need self-hostable Copilot replacements.
Combined with Devstral (agentic) and Codestral Mamba (long
context), Mistral covers the IDE coding / repo-level agent
spectrum without depending on US-hosted weights.
public_view.why_it_matters:
Codestral helped commoditize the "coding-specialized model"
category that previously belonged to GitHub Copilot and DeepSeek-
Coder. Now every major lab ships a coding variant.

nodes.body_md:
## Mistral's code stack

| Model | Date | Niche |
|---|---|---|
| Codestral 22B | May 2024 | First Mistral coding model |
| **Codestral 25.01** | Jan 2025 | Refresh; stronger FIM |
| Codestral Mamba 7B | 2024 | State-space; long context |
| Devstral 24B | 2025 | Agentic coding (with All Hands AI) |

nodes.title: MiniMax-Text-01 — Lightning Attention at 4M Context

model_spec.architecture: MoE + hybrid Lightning Attention (linear) + softmax attention every 8 layers

benchmarks.name: Needle-in-haystack (4M context)
benchmarks.vs_baseline: first model to ship 4M-token context publicly

benchmarks.name: RULER (long-context, 1M)
benchmarks.score: competitive with Gemini 1.5 Pro on documented subsets

public_view.plain_english:
A Shanghai startup's January 2025 open-weight model with a 4
million-token context window — the longest publicly available
at the time, twice Kimi's 2M and four times Gemini 1.5 Pro's 1M.
Achieved this by replacing most layers' standard attention with
"Lightning Attention" — a linear-time variant — and keeping
standard attention only every 8th layer to preserve quality on
short-context tasks.
public_view.analogy:
Where most LLMs are sprinters that get tired by the end of a
marathon, MiniMax-Text-01 changes the running technique itself
so it can keep going for 4× the distance — at some cost to peak
speed in the first kilometer.
public_view.investment_angle:
MiniMax raised >$1B at $2.5B+ valuation by 2024 as one of China's
"AI tigers." The MiniMax-Text-01 release was the company's first
open-weight foundation model — a strategic pivot from purely
closed consumer-product positioning. The Lightning Attention
architecture is a real technical contribution: most labs trying
to push beyond 1M tokens have hit quadratic-attention scaling
walls, and MiniMax's linear-attention hybrid demonstrated a
practical path forward. Anthropic's later 1M-context Claude
Opus 4.6 (and rumors of larger contexts in 2026) likely use
related techniques.
public_view.why_it_matters:
MiniMax-Text-01 is the proof point that >>1M context isn't just
a Google-DeepMind-scale-only feature — it's achievable with
architectural changes that any frontier lab can adopt. The
"context arms race" of 2024-25 has converged on hybrid linear /
standard attention as the default approach, with MiniMax as one
of the early demonstrations.

citations.title: MiniMax-01: Scaling Foundation Models with Lightning Attention

nodes.body_md:
## What it is

A 456-billion-parameter open-weight Mixture-of-Experts model with 45.9B active parameters, released January 15, 2025. The architectural headline: most attention layers use "Lightning Attention" — a chunked linear-time attention variant — with standard quadratic softmax attention preserved only on every 8th layer to maintain short-context fidelity.

This hybrid achieves O(n) instead of O(n²) cost in sequence length for the linear-attention layers, enabling the practical 4M-token context window. At 4M tokens, standard quadratic attention would require ~16TB of attention memory — effectively impossible. Linear attention requires ~constant memory per token.

## How MiniMax-Text-01 compares to long-context peers

| Model | Date | Org | Context | Approach |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPT-4 | 2023-03 | OpenAI | 8k / 32k | standard attention |
| Claude 2.1 | 2023-11 | Anthropic | 200k | standard + memory tricks |
| Gemini 1.5 Pro | 2024-02 | Google | 1M (10M experimental) | undisclosed; likely hybrid |
| Kimi (March update) | 2024-03 | Moonshot | 2M | undisclosed |
| Claude 3 family | 2024-03 | Anthropic | 200k | standard + memory tricks |
| GPT-4 Turbo | 2023-11 | OpenAI | 128k | standard |
| **MiniMax-Text-01** | **2025-01** | **MiniMax** | **4M** | **Lightning Attention hybrid (open)** |
| Claude Opus 4.6 (1M variant) | 2025-12 | Anthropic | 1M | undisclosed |

MiniMax's contribution beyond the headline number: by open-sourcing the architecture, they made the technique available for other labs to study and adapt.

nodes.title: MatterGen — Generative Materials Discovery

model_spec.architecture: Diffusion model on crystal structure

public_view.plain_english:
Microsoft's January 2025 generative model for inorganic materials
— generates novel crystal structures with target properties
(band gap, magnetism, ionic conductivity). Published in Nature.
Combined with Microsoft's MatterSim (property simulator),
forms a closed-loop materials design system. Used by partners
in battery, semiconductor, and catalyst R&D.
public_view.analogy:
What RFdiffusion did for proteins, MatterGen does for crystals —
"design me a material with these properties" instead of "find
one in a database."
public_view.investment_angle:
Materials discovery is a 5-10 year R&D problem; AI-acceleration
tools like MatterGen are bets that the next-decade breakthroughs
in batteries / solar / catalysts will originate in AI-designed
